news_summary: Today's technology news highlights significant advancements in cutting-edge science and innovation, including breakthroughs in fusion, quantum research, particle physics, major global investments in scientific funding, and groundbreaking paleontological discoveries.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans les domaines de la science de pointe et de l'innovation, notamment des percées dans la fusion nucléaire, la recherche quantique et la physique des particules, ainsi que d'importants investissements mondiaux dans le financement de la recherche scientifique et des découvertes paléontologiques révolutionnaires.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan importantes avances en ciencia e innovación de vanguardia, entre los que se incluyen avances en fusión nuclear, investigación cuántica y física de partículas, así como importantes inversiones mundiales en financiación científica y descubrimientos paleontológicos revolucionarios.

articles.title: UK bets big on homegrown fusion and quantum — can it lead the world?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00877-2UK government announces multibillion-pound science investments — but what impact will this have on the global race in these fields?
articles.content:
Rachel Reeves, UK chancellor the exchequer (left), and Patrick Vallance, UK science minister (centre), speak with an employee during a visit to the Siemens Healthineers factory.Credit: Chris Ratcliffe/Bloomberg via Getty

Britain is making an ambitious technological bet. It is investing £2 billion (US$2.66 billion) in quantum-computing development and £2.5 billion in nuclear-fusion energy in a bid to secure technological and energy independence and nurture homegrown scientific talent.

The changes — announced on 16 March as part of an ongoing national science and technology strategy — have been broadly welcomed by the research community. And officials say that the money and increased strategic focus will help to push the United Kingdom to the forefront of both fields globally.

However, some point out that long-term commitments and more money will be needed if Britain is to push past its competitors. Others lament that the funding is not so much heightened ambition as necessary merely to maintain aspects of the nation’s current scientific capabilities given the disruptive effects Brexit had on its science funding and access to joint European projects.

For example, the United Kingdom withdrew from the International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER), a long-running international effort to build an experimental fusion reactor in France.

“You have to go back to Brexit to understand what’s going on now,” says Tony Roulstone, a nuclear-power researcher at the University of Cambridge, UK.

Boost to quantum computing

Officials say that the quantum investment will lay the foundations for the United Kingdom to become the first country to roll out the large-scale use of quantum computers and be the fastest to adopt artificial intelligence in the G7 group of nations.

The £2-billion quantum package aims to support research, infrastructure, skills and commercialization, including funding for hardware and software development, expanded facilities and support for start-ups and industry partnerships.

Quantum computers: what are they good for?

The government has also pledged to buy and use successful systems as they emerge — echoing the procurement mechanisms used by the United States to promote the development of satellite navigation systems and stealth aircraft.

But Britain faces stiff competition globally. Large-scale quantum computing — systems that offer consistent, practical advantages across multiple sectors — is not yet possible.

Word’s first fusion?

The £2.5-billion fusion investment is similarly ambitious — although how it will compete on a global stage is also unclear. The funds include plans to build a prototype fusion energy plant called Spherical Tokamak for Energy Production (STEP) on the site of a former coal-fired power station in the centre of the United Kingdom. They also include £45 million for building the nation’s first AI supercomputer dedicated to accelerating fusion-energy research.

Researchers say that STEP is a ‘moonshot’ project, a high-risk initiative that might not prove successful but could still spark scientific breakthroughs. It’s aim of producing significantly more power output than the total input – a key requirement for fusion energy – is extremely ambitious.

“It will build a lot of capacity in material science, in magnet engineering, all sorts of things,” says Richard Jones, an experimental physicist who retired last year from the University of Manchester, UK.
articles.reasoning: The UK government is making a substantial investment in quantum computing and nuclear fusion, aiming to advance scientific capabilities and energy independence with broad societal and technological benefits. The article provides detailed context on the scale, goals, and potential impact of these investments, highlighting their significance for the UK and the global technology landscape.
articles.personality_title: UK invests billions in quantum computing and fusion energy to lead science innovation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The UK government is focusing on science and technology to boost its future. It plans to spend large amounts of money on two advanced fields: quantum computing and nuclear fusion energy. These areas are known for their potential to change how we use computers and produce clean energy.

**What happened** – On March 16, 2026, the UK announced it will invest £2 billion in quantum computing and £2.5 billion in nuclear fusion. The fusion project includes building a new experimental plant called STEP on a former coal power site. The government also plans to support research, develop new technologies, and help businesses in these fields.

**Impact** – This is a big step because quantum computers can solve problems much faster than regular computers, and fusion energy could provide a nearly unlimited clean power source. The UK hopes to become a world leader, especially in the G7 group of rich countries. However, experts say the projects are risky and will need more time and money to beat other countries that are working on the same technologies.

**What’s next step** – The UK will start building the fusion plant and create better facilities for quantum research. Officials will also buy and use new quantum computers as they are developed. The success of these projects could lead to new jobs, cleaner energy, and faster computers in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UK is investing billions to develop quantum computing and fusion energy, aiming to lead global science and technology innovation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Royaume-Uni investit des milliards dans l'informatique quantique et la fusion nucléaire pour diriger l'innovation scientifique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le gouvernement britannique se concentre sur la science et la technologie pour son avenir. Il prévoit de dépenser beaucoup d'argent dans deux domaines avancés : l'informatique quantique et l'énergie de fusion nucléaire. Ces domaines ont le potentiel de changer l'utilisation des ordinateurs et la production d'énergie propre.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 16 mars 2026, le Royaume-Uni a annoncé un investissement de 2 milliards de livres dans l'informatique quantique et de 2,5 milliards dans la fusion nucléaire. Le projet de fusion inclut la construction d'une nouvelle centrale expérimentale appelée STEP sur un ancien site de centrale à charbon. Le gouvernement soutiendra aussi la recherche, développera de nouvelles technologies et aidera les entreprises dans ces domaines.

**Impact** – C’est une étape importante car les ordinateurs quantiques peuvent résoudre des problèmes beaucoup plus rapidement que les ordinateurs classiques, et l'énergie de fusion pourrait fournir une source d'énergie propre presque illimitée. Le Royaume-Uni espère devenir un leader mondial, surtout parmi les pays du G7. Cependant, les experts disent que ces projets sont risqués et nécessiteront plus de temps et d'argent pour surpasser les autres pays.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le Royaume-Uni commencera à construire la centrale de fusion et à créer de meilleures infrastructures pour la recherche quantique. Les responsables achèteront et utiliseront aussi les nouveaux ordinateurs quantiques au fur et à mesure de leur développement. Le succès de ces projets pourrait créer de nouveaux emplois, une énergie plus propre et des ordinateurs plus rapides.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le Royaume-Uni investit des milliards pour développer l'informatique quantique et l'énergie de fusion, visant à devenir un leader mondial en innovation scientifique et technologique.
articles.personality_title_es: Reino Unido invierte miles de millones en computación cuántica y energía de fusión para liderar la innovación científica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El gobierno del Reino Unido está enfocándose en la ciencia y tecnología para su futuro. Planea gastar grandes sumas de dinero en dos áreas avanzadas: computación cuántica y energía de fusión nuclear. Estos campos tienen el potencial de cambiar cómo usamos las computadoras y producimos energía limpia.

**Qué pasó** – El 16 de marzo de 2026, el Reino Unido anunció que invertirá £2 mil millones en computación cuántica y £2.5 mil millones en energía de fusión. El proyecto de fusión incluye construir una planta experimental llamada STEP en un antiguo sitio de una central de carbón. El gobierno también apoyará la investigación, desarrollará nuevas tecnologías y ayudará a empresas en estos campos.

**Impacto** – Esto es un gran paso porque las computadoras cuánticas pueden resolver problemas mucho más rápido que las normales, y la energía de fusión podría brindar una fuente de energía limpia casi ilimitada. El Reino Unido espera ser líder mundial, especialmente en el grupo G7. Sin embargo, los expertos dicen que los proyectos son riesgosos y necesitarán más tiempo y dinero para superar a otros países que trabajan en tecnologías similares.

**Próximo paso** – El Reino Unido comenzará a construir la planta de fusión y mejorará las instalaciones para la investigación cuántica. Los funcionarios también comprarán y usarán nuevas computadoras cuánticas a medida que se desarrollen. El éxito de estos proyectos podría crear nuevos empleos, energía más limpia y computadoras más rápidas en el futuro.

**Frase clave** – El Reino Unido invierte miles de millones para desarrollar computación cuántica y energía de fusión, con la meta de liderar la innovación científica y tecnológica global.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing spherical tokamak fusion reactor at the center of a peaceful British countryside, surrounded by abstract, softly illuminated quantum circuits and interconnected nodes gently rising like mist, symbolizing cutting-edge fusion energy and quantum computing innovation emerging from the UK landscape.

articles.title: Physicists discover a heavy cousin of the proton at CERN’s Large Hadron Collider
articles.summary: A new subatomic particle known as the Ξcc⁺ has been discovered at CERN’s Large Hadron Collider. This heavy proton-like particle contains two charm quarks and was detected using the upgraded LHCb experiment. Scientists observed it through its decay into lighter particles in high-energy collisions. The finding confirms predictions and settles a decades-long question about its existence.
articles.content:
Scientists from The University of Manchester have played a key role in identifying a previously unknown subatomic particle at CERN's Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The particle, called the Ξ cc ⁺ (Xi-cc-plus), is a heavy proton-like particle made up of two charm quarks and one down quark.

This marks the first particle discovery using the upgraded LHCb detector. The upgrade is part of a major international effort involving more than 1,000 researchers across 20 countries. The United Kingdom contributed more than any other nation, with Manchester providing significant leadership.

A Heavier Relative of the Proton

The newly discovered Ξ cc ⁺ is part of the same family as the proton, which was first identified in Manchester by Ernest Rutherford and colleagues between 1917-1919. While a proton contains two up quarks and one down quark, the Ξ cc ⁺ replaces the up quarks with heavier charm quarks.

This finding also builds on a long history of particle physics research at Manchester. In the 1950s, scientists at the university were the first to identify a member of the Ξ (Xi) particle family, laying the groundwork for discoveries like this one.

Manchester's Role in the LHCb Detector Upgrade

Professor Chris Parkes, head of the University's Department of Physics and Astronomy, led the international collaboration during the installation and early operation of the upgraded LHCb detector. He also oversaw the United Kingdom's involvement in the project for more than ten years, guiding it from initial approval through completion.

The Manchester LHCb team designed and built essential parts of the upgraded tracking system, including silicon pixel detector modules assembled in the University's Schuster Building. These components are critical for accurately tracking particle decays and identifying signals such as the Ξ cc ⁺.

Professor Parkes, said, "Rutherford's gold-foil experiment in a Manchester basement transformed our understanding of matter, and today's discovery builds on that legacy using state-of-the-art technology at CERN. Both milestones demonstrate just how far curiosity driven research can take us. This discovery showcases the extraordinary capability of the upgraded LHCb detector and the strength of UK and Manchester contributions to the experiment."

Advanced Detector Captures Particle Collisions

Dr. Stefano De Capua from The University of Manchester led the production of the silicon detector modules. He described the detector as operating like a high-speed camera.

"The detector is a form of 'camera' that images the particles produced at the LHC and takes photographs 40 million times per second. It utilises a custom-designed silicon chip that also has a variant for use in medical imaging applications."

How the Ξ cc ⁺ Particle Was Identified

Researchers detected the Ξ cc ⁺ by observing how it decays into three lighter particles (Λ c ⁺ K⁻ π⁺). These decay events were recorded during proton-proton collisions at the LHC in 2024, the first year the upgraded LHCb experiment ran at full capacity.

A clear signal of about 915 events was measured at a mass of 3619.97 MeV/c2. This result matches predictions based on a previously discovered related particle, the Ξ cc ⁺⁺.

Solving a Two-Decade Mystery in Particle Physics

For more than 20 years, scientists had debated earlier claims that this particle had been observed, but those findings were never confirmed. The new measurement from LHCb places the particle at a mass that does not match the earlier claim but does agree with theoretical expectations based on its partner particle.

What Comes Next for CERN and Manchester

Looking ahead, The University of Manchester will continue to play a leading role in the next phase of the LHC program, known as LHCb Upgrade 2. This upgrade will take advantage of the High-Luminosity LHC accelerator to gather more data and explore rare particles in greater detail.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a new subatomic particle at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, confirming long-standing theoretical predictions and advancing fundamental physics knowledge. The discovery has broad scientific significance, involves an international collaboration, and showcases technological innovation with the upgraded LHCb detector. It is a focused, substantive story about a breakthrough with potential long-term impact on particle physics.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover new heavy particle at CERN’s Large Hadron Collider
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been studying tiny particles called quarks that make up protons and neutrons inside atoms. The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN is a giant machine that smashes particles together to find new ones and learn how the universe works.

**What happened** – Researchers at CERN, with important help from the University of Manchester, found a new particle named Ξcc⁺ (Xi-cc-plus) in 2024. This particle is like a heavier cousin of the proton because it contains two charm quarks and one down quark. It was spotted using a new, improved detector called LHCb, which can track particles very quickly and precisely.

**Impact** – This discovery confirms a particle that scientists had predicted but never clearly seen before. It solves a question that lasted over 20 years about whether this particle really exists. The finding also shows how advanced the upgraded LHCb detector is and highlights the important role of Manchester and the UK in building and operating this technology.

**What's next step** – The University of Manchester and other teams will keep working on the next upgrade called LHCb Upgrade 2. This will allow scientists to collect even more data and study rare particles better, helping us understand more about the building blocks of matter.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new heavy particle similar to the proton was discovered at CERN, confirming decades-old predictions and showcasing advanced technology developed with key contributions from the University of Manchester.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une nouvelle particule lourde au Grand collisionneur de hadrons du CERN
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des particules très petites appelées quarks qui composent les protons et les neutrons dans les atomes. Le Grand collisionneur de hadrons (LHC) du CERN est une énorme machine qui fait entrer en collision des particules pour en découvrir de nouvelles et comprendre l’univers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, des chercheurs du CERN, avec une aide importante de l’Université de Manchester, ont découvert une nouvelle particule nommée Ξcc⁺ (Xi-cc-plus). Cette particule est comme une cousine plus lourde du proton car elle contient deux quarks charme et un quark bas. Elle a été détectée grâce à un détecteur amélioré appelé LHCb, capable de suivre les particules très rapidement et précisément.

**Impact** – Cette découverte confirme une particule que les scientifiques avaient prévue mais jamais clairement observée. Elle résout une question vieille de plus de 20 ans sur son existence. Cette réussite montre aussi la puissance du détecteur LHCb amélioré et souligne le rôle important de Manchester et du Royaume-Uni dans la construction et l’utilisation de cette technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – L’Université de Manchester et d’autres équipes continueront à travailler sur la prochaine mise à niveau appelée LHCb Upgrade 2. Cela permettra de collecter encore plus de données et d’étudier mieux les particules rares, pour mieux comprendre les éléments de base de la matière.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle particule lourde similaire au proton a été découverte au CERN, confirmant des prédictions vieilles de plusieurs décennies et mettant en valeur une technologie avancée développée avec une contribution clé de l’Université de Manchester.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren una nueva partícula pesada en el Gran Colisionador de Hadrones del CERN
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian partículas muy pequeñas llamadas quarks que forman los protones y neutrones dentro de los átomos. El Gran Colisionador de Hadrones (LHC) en el CERN es una máquina enorme que choca partículas para encontrar nuevas y entender cómo funciona el universo.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, investigadores del CERN, con ayuda importante de la Universidad de Manchester, descubrieron una nueva partícula llamada Ξcc⁺ (Xi-cc-plus). Esta partícula es como una prima más pesada del protón porque tiene dos quarks encanto y un quark down. Fue detectada usando un detector mejorado llamado LHCb, que puede seguir partículas muy rápido y con precisión.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo confirma una partícula que los científicos habían predicho pero nunca habían visto claramente. Resuelve una duda que duró más de 20 años sobre su existencia. También muestra lo avanzado que es el detector mejorado LHCb y destaca el papel importante de Manchester y el Reino Unido en construir y usar esta tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – La Universidad de Manchester y otros equipos seguirán trabajando en la próxima mejora llamada LHCb Upgrade 2. Esto permitirá recoger más datos y estudiar partículas raras con mayor detalle para entender mejor los bloques básicos de la materia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Se descubrió una nueva partícula pesada similar al protón en el CERN, confirmando predicciones de hace décadas y mostrando tecnología avanzada desarrollada con una contribución clave de la Universidad de Manchester.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized atom with three distinct quarks—two glowing charm quarks and one softer down quark—intertwined at its center, surrounded by delicate, abstract representations of silicon detector modules resembling intricate circuit patterns, all set against a softly illuminated backdrop evoking the Large Hadron Collider’s circular tunnel.

articles.title: China could be the world’s biggest public funder of science within two years
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00618-5Forecast by science-policy researchers raises questions about where the epicentre of global research will shift to in the coming decade.
articles.content:
The Centrifugal Hyper-gravity and Interdisciplinary Experiment Facility in Hangzhou is one of several major science infrastructure projects to be funded by China’s national government.Credit: VCG via Getty

China is on the cusp of becoming the world’s biggest public funder of research, according to a forecast by US academics, as stalled growth in government investment in the United States coincides with consistent rises in spending by the Chinese authorities.

The analysis — produced exclusively for Nature Index — was the work of researchers from Frontiers in Science and Innovation Policy (FSIP), a programme at the University of California, San Diego, that studies the US research and development (R&D) system and examines the extent to which public and private funding boost technological development.

Government spending on R&D in China increased by 90% to US$133 billion in the decade leading up to 2023, according to the most recent purchasing-power-adjusted data from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). By contrast, in the United States, spending rose by just 12%, to $155 billion.

According to the FSIP’s forecast, China’s public spending on research is likely to overtake that of the United States in the next two to three years.

“I think the earliest likely is 2028, plus [or] minus one year,” says Robert Conn, a specialist in research policy and science philanthropy, who co-leads the FSIP. “It could be next year, could be 2029.”

The United States has been the global leader in R&D investment since the end of the Second World War. China taking the lead in public research spending would therefore be a watershed moment. It would set the scene for the emergence of a new “hegemon” in science, says Meghan Ostertag, who studies economic policy at the Information Technology and Innovation Foundation, a think tank in Washington DC.

In the past few years, China has already pulled ahead of the United States on various measures of research performance. In the 145 natural-science and health-science journals tracked by Nature Index, for instance, its contribution is on track to be double that of the United States by the end of 2026.

The FSIP projection is, in some ways, conservative. It predicts that US research spending will remain flat, despite efforts by the administration of President Donald Trump to cut research budgets. At the same time, it incorporates a slowdown in Chinese research spending earlier in this decade, explains Christopher Martin, president of the non-profit organization Explorative Science Foundation in Christchurch, New Zealand, who worked on the analysis for the FSIP.

Martin looked at the growth trajectory in spending from 2020 to 2023 and used that to extrapolate forwards. The annual growth rate of China’s government spending was between 1% and 5% after 2020, compared with an average of 11% between 2005 and 2020.

“My best guess is that the 2020 pandemic made policymakers more conservative,” he explains, “which was then exacerbated by the Chinese property-sector crisis beginning in 2021.” If he had gone back further, China’s upward trajectory “might have been a little more steep” (see ‘Crossover point’).

Earlier this month, the Chinese government announced proposals for the country’s total annual research spending, from all sources, to increase by at least 7% per year for the coming five-year period from 2026 to 2030.

Yutao Sun, a specialist in innovation policy at Dalian University of Technology in China, thinks that the government's research spending might increase even more, which would bring the crossover point with the United States even closer.

Fundamental research

In terms of spending on fundamental research, China still has a long way to go. Its overall 2023 expenditure in this area, from both private and public sources, was $53 billion — less than half that of the United States, which stood at $120 billion, according to the most recent purchasing-power-adjusted data from the OECD.

Yet Chinese government spending on fundamental research more than tripled between 2013 and 2023, whereas it increased by just over half in the United States, when comparing data on central and regional government expenditure from the Chinese Ministry of Finance and the US National Center for Science and Engineering Statistics.

In the United States, the FSIP expects overall funding for fundamental research agencies, such as the National Science Foundation and the National Institutes of Health, to be broadly flat this year as the US Senate pushes back on cuts requested by the Trump administration.

However, over the coming years, the FSIP team thinks that government spending on fundamental research in the United States is likely to decline in real terms, and that Congress will be unable to fully push back against cuts requested by the White House. This puts China on track to surpass the United States in this area “within a decade”, according to Conn.

Because fundamental research sits at the beginning of the development process, it represents the “seed corn that will provide the innovations and discoveries a decade from now”, explains Conn. “That’s where we’re really going to falter, and that’s where the biggest changes tend to occur.” (See ‘Fundamental gap’.)

Broader goals

China’s increased focus on fundamental and applied research is part of a “grander strategy” by the Chinese government to be a world leader economically and politically, says Ostertag. “I think years and years ago they saw that technology and science was the way to do that, and I think the rest of the world is realizing that now.”

Sun cautions that the FSIP analysis is based on OECD data adjusted by purchasing power, which takes into account the fact that goods and services in China are cheaper than they are in the United States, meaning that money goes further. Sun says that the analysis might be overstating Chinese research spending as a result, because items such as scientific equipment and journal access are often purchased on the international markets.

But if there is any slowdown in government spending on research in the United States, it might have long-term implications for business growth. A 2024 analysis by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas in Texas estimates that public funding has accounted for one-fifth of the growth in US business-sector productivity since the Second World War, because it spurs private research spending and increases innovation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and positive shift in global public funding for scientific research, with China poised to become the largest public funder of science. This shift has broad implications for global innovation, technological development, and economic growth, benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context about funding trends, comparative data, and future projections, making it a substantive and meaningful story about scientific progress and investment.
articles.personality_title: China set to become top public funder of science within two years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, the United States has led the world in government spending on scientific research. This funding supports discoveries and new technologies that help economies grow and improve lives.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of California, San Diego, predict that China will surpass the United States in public funding for science within the next two to three years. China’s government spending on research grew by 90% over the past decade, reaching $133 billion in 2023, while the US spending increased by only 12% to $155 billion. China also plans to increase its research budget by at least 7% annually from 2026 to 2030.

**Impact** – This change marks a big shift in global science leadership. China already produces twice as many research papers as the US in top science journals. Although China still spends less on basic research—the early stage that leads to future discoveries—it is increasing that spending faster than the US. This could affect which country leads in new technologies and scientific breakthroughs in the future.

**What’s next step** – If China continues to raise its research funding, it may become the world leader in fundamental science within a decade. Meanwhile, US funding might stay flat or even decrease, which could influence innovation and business growth. Scientists and policymakers worldwide will watch closely how this shift shapes global science and technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – China is quickly increasing its government investment in science and is expected to become the world’s largest public funder of research within a few years, signaling a major change in global scientific leadership.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Chine devrait devenir le premier financeur public de la science d’ici deux ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les États-Unis ont été les premiers au monde en dépenses publiques pour la recherche scientifique. Ce financement soutient les découvertes et les nouvelles technologies qui font avancer les économies et améliorent la vie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à San Diego prévoient que la Chine dépassera les États-Unis en financement public de la science dans les deux à trois prochaines années. Les dépenses publiques chinoises pour la recherche ont augmenté de 90 % en dix ans, atteignant 133 milliards de dollars en 2023, tandis que celles des États-Unis ont augmenté de seulement 12 %, à 155 milliards de dollars. La Chine prévoit aussi d’augmenter son budget de recherche d’au moins 7 % par an entre 2026 et 2030.

**Impact** – Ce changement marque un tournant important dans le leadership mondial en science. La Chine produit déjà deux fois plus d’articles dans les meilleurs journaux scientifiques que les États-Unis. Bien que la Chine dépense encore moins pour la recherche fondamentale – la phase initiale qui mène aux futures découvertes – elle augmente ces dépenses plus rapidement que les États-Unis. Cela pourrait influencer quel pays dominera dans les nouvelles technologies et les avancées scientifiques à l’avenir.

**Prochaine étape** – Si la Chine continue à augmenter son financement de la recherche, elle pourrait devenir leader mondial en science fondamentale dans la prochaine décennie. Pendant ce temps, le financement américain pourrait stagner ou diminuer, ce qui pourrait affecter l’innovation et la croissance économique. Les scientifiques et décideurs surveilleront de près comment ce changement influencera la science et la technologie dans le monde.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La Chine augmente rapidement son investissement public dans la science et devrait devenir le plus grand financeur mondial de la recherche dans quelques années, marquant un changement majeur dans le leadership scientifique mondial.
articles.personality_title_es: China se convertirá en el mayor financiador público de ciencia en dos años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, Estados Unidos ha liderado el mundo en gasto público para la investigación científica. Este financiamiento apoya descubrimientos y nuevas tecnologías que impulsan las economías y mejoran la vida.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en San Diego predicen que China superará a Estados Unidos en financiamiento público para la ciencia dentro de dos a tres años. El gasto público chino en investigación creció un 90 % en la última década, alcanzando 133 mil millones de dólares en 2023, mientras que el gasto de EE. UU. aumentó solo un 12 % hasta 155 mil millones. China también planea aumentar su presupuesto de investigación al menos un 7 % anual de 2026 a 2030.

**Impacto** – Este cambio representa un gran giro en el liderazgo científico mundial. China ya produce el doble de artículos en las principales revistas científicas que Estados Unidos. Aunque China todavía gasta menos en investigación básica —la etapa inicial que conduce a futuros descubrimientos— está aumentando ese gasto más rápido que EE. UU. Esto podría afectar qué país lidera en nuevas tecnologías y avances científicos en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – Si China continúa aumentando su financiamiento, podría convertirse en líder mundial en ciencia fundamental dentro de una década. Mientras tanto, el financiamiento en EE. UU. podría mantenerse estable o disminuir, lo que podría influir en la innovación y el crecimiento empresarial. Científicos y responsables políticos observarán de cerca cómo este cambio afecta la ciencia y la tecnología global.

**Conclusión en una frase** – China está aumentando rápidamente su inversión pública en ciencia y se espera que se convierta en el mayor financiador mundial de investigación en pocos años, señalando un cambio importante en el liderazgo científico global.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, intricate scientific research facility shaped like a stylized centrifuge tower glowing warmly with soft golden and red light, set against a calm, expansive skyline symbolizing progress and growth, with abstract rising bars or beams of light representing increasing government investment illuminating the scene, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and gentle reds without any human figures.

articles.title: Scientists recreated a dinosaur nest to solve a 70-million-year-old mystery
articles.summary: Scientists recreated a life-size oviraptor nest to understand how these dinosaurs hatched their eggs. Their experiments showed the parent likely couldn’t heat all the eggs directly, meaning sunlight played a key role. This uneven heating could cause eggs in the same nest to hatch at different times. The results suggest oviraptors used a hybrid incubation method unlike modern birds.
articles.content:
How oviraptors, bird-like but flightless dinosaurs, hatched their eggs has long been unclear. Did they depend on heat from their surroundings like crocodiles, or did they warm their eggs directly like birds? A new study in Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution explores this question by examining oviraptor nesting behavior and hatching patterns.

Researchers in Taiwan combined heat transfer simulations with physical experiments to better understand how these dinosaurs incubated their eggs. They also compared their findings with modern bird incubation. To do this, they built a life size model of an oviraptor and a realistic nest to test how heat moved through the eggs.

"We show the difference in oviraptor hatching patterns was induced by the relative position of the incubating adult to the eggs," said senior author Dr. Tzu-Ruei Yang, an associate curator of vertebrate paleontology at Taiwan's National Museum of Natural Science.

"Moreover, we obtained an estimate of the incubation efficiency of oviraptors, which is much lower than that of modern birds," added first author Chun-Yu Su, who attended Washington High School in Taichung when the research was conducted.

Reconstructing an Oviraptor Nest

The model was based on Heyuannia huangi, an oviraptor species that lived between 70 and 66 million years ago in what is now China. This dinosaur was about 1.5 meters long and weighed around 20kg. It built semi-open nests arranged in multiple rings of eggs.

To recreate the animal, researchers constructed the torso using polystyrene foam and a wooden frame, then added cotton, bubble paper, and fabric to mimic soft tissues. The eggs were made from casting resin. In the experiments, two clutches were arranged in double rings to match fossil evidence.

"Part of the difficulty lies in reconstructing oviraptor incubation realistically," said Su. "For example, their eggs are unlike those of any living species, so we invented the resin eggs to approximate real oviraptor eggs as best as we could."

Heat, Nest Design, and Hatching Patterns

The team tested how both adult presence and environmental conditions affected egg temperatures and hatching outcomes.

In colder conditions, when a brooding adult was present, temperatures in the outer ring of eggs varied by as much as 6°C. Such differences could lead to asynchronous hatching, where eggs in the same nest hatch at different times. In warmer environments, that variation dropped to about 0.6°C. This suggests that in warmer climates, sunlight may have helped even out temperatures and influenced hatching patterns.

"It's unlikely that large dinosaurs sat atop their clutches. Supposedly, they used the heat of the sun or soil to hatch their eggs, like turtles. Since oviraptor clutches are open to the air, heat from the sun likely mattered much more than heat from the soil," Yang explained.

Dinosaur vs Bird Incubation Efficiency

The researchers also compared oviraptor incubation to that of modern birds. Most birds rely on thermoregulatory contact incubation (TCI), where adults sit directly on their eggs and provide heat. For TCI to work, the adult must touch all the eggs, act as the main heat source, and keep temperatures consistent.

Oviraptors likely could not meet these conditions. Their ring shaped egg arrangement meant the adult could not maintain contact with every egg at once.

"Oviraptors may not have been able to conduct TCI as modern birds do," said Su. Instead, these dinosaurs and environmental heat likely worked together, making them co-incubators. While this method was less efficient than that of modern birds, it may have been well suited to their nesting style, which appears to have shifted from buried nests to semi-open ones.

"Modern birds aren't 'better' at hatching eggs. Instead, birds living today and oviraptors have a very different way of incubation or, more specifically, brooding," Yang pointed out. "Nothing is better or worse. It just depends on the environment."

What This Means for Dinosaur Parenting

The researchers caution that their results are based on a reconstructed nest and modern environmental conditions, which differ from those of the Late Cretaceous. These differences could influence the findings. They also note that oviraptors likely had longer incubation periods than modern birds.

Even with these limitations, the study provides new insight into how oviraptors may have cared for their eggs. By combining physical models with simulations, the work opens new possibilities for studying dinosaur reproduction.

"It also truly is an encouragement for all students, especially in Taiwan," concluded Yang. "There are no dinosaur fossils in Taiwan, but that does not mean that we cannot do dinosaur studies."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement where researchers recreated a dinosaur nest to solve a long-standing paleontological mystery about dinosaur incubation methods. This breakthrough provides meaningful insights into dinosaur reproductive behavior, combining innovative physical modeling and simulations, which has broad educational and scientific value beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists recreate dinosaur nest to reveal how oviraptors hatched eggs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how oviraptors, dinosaurs that looked like birds but couldn’t fly, kept their eggs warm. Unlike modern birds that sit on eggs to warm them, it was unclear if oviraptors used heat from their bodies or from the environment.

**What happened** – Researchers in Taiwan built a full-size model of an oviraptor and its nest, based on fossils. They used fake eggs and tested how heat moved through the nest with and without an adult nearby. Their experiments showed that the adult dinosaur likely could not warm all eggs directly, so sunlight and the environment helped heat the eggs unevenly.

**Impact** – This study explains why eggs in the same nest might hatch at different times. It shows oviraptors used a mix of body heat and environmental heat to incubate eggs, unlike modern birds that rely mainly on direct contact. This finding helps us understand dinosaur parenting and shows they had a unique way of caring for their eggs.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use these new methods to study other dinosaur species and improve knowledge about how different dinosaurs raised their young. This research also encourages students everywhere to explore paleontology, even in places without dinosaur fossils.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By recreating an oviraptor nest, scientists discovered these dinosaurs used both their body heat and sunlight to hatch eggs, revealing a unique incubation method different from modern birds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques recréent un nid de dinosaure pour comprendre l’éclosion des œufs d’oviraptors
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se sont longtemps demandé comment les oviraptors, des dinosaures ressemblant à des oiseaux mais incapables de voler, réchauffaient leurs œufs. Contrairement aux oiseaux modernes qui couvent leurs œufs, on ignorait si les oviraptors utilisaient la chaleur de leur corps ou celle de l’environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs à Taïwan ont construit un modèle grandeur nature d’un oviraptor et de son nid, basé sur des fossiles. Ils ont utilisé de faux œufs et testé comment la chaleur se répartissait dans le nid, avec ou sans la présence d’un adulte. Leurs expériences ont montré que l’adulte ne pouvait probablement pas réchauffer tous les œufs directement, donc la lumière du soleil et l’environnement contribuaient au chauffage inégal des œufs.

**Impact** – Cette étude explique pourquoi les œufs du même nid pouvaient éclore à des moments différents. Elle montre que les oviraptors utilisaient un mélange de chaleur corporelle et de chaleur environnementale pour incubuer leurs œufs, contrairement aux oiseaux modernes qui comptent surtout sur le contact direct. Cette découverte éclaire la façon dont ces dinosaures s’occupaient de leurs œufs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques comptent utiliser ces nouvelles méthodes pour étudier d’autres espèces de dinosaures et mieux comprendre comment ils élevaient leurs petits. Cette recherche encourage aussi les étudiants, même dans les régions sans fossiles de dinosaures, à s’intéresser à la paléontologie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En recréant un nid d’oviraptor, les scientifiques ont découvert que ces dinosaures utilisaient à la fois leur chaleur corporelle et la lumière du soleil pour faire éclore leurs œufs, révélant une méthode d’incubation unique différente de celle des oiseaux modernes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos recrean nido de dinosaurio para descubrir cómo incubaban sus huevos los ovirraptores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos se han preguntado cómo los ovirraptores, dinosaurios parecidos a aves pero que no volaban, mantenían calientes sus huevos. A diferencia de las aves modernas que se sientan sobre sus huevos, no estaba claro si los ovirraptores usaban calor corporal o el calor del ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Taiwán construyeron un modelo a tamaño real de un ovirraptor y su nido, basado en fósiles. Usaron huevos falsos y probaron cómo se distribuía el calor en el nido con y sin la presencia del adulto. Sus experimentos mostraron que probablemente el adulto no podía calentar todos los huevos directamente, por lo que la luz del sol y el ambiente ayudaban a calentar los huevos de manera desigual.

**Impacto** – Este estudio explica por qué los huevos en el mismo nido podían eclosionar en momentos diferentes. Muestra que los ovirraptores usaban una mezcla de calor corporal y ambiental para incubar sus huevos, diferente a las aves modernas que dependen principalmente del contacto directo. Este hallazgo ayuda a entender cómo cuidaban sus huevos estos dinosaurios.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar estos nuevos métodos para estudiar otras especies de dinosaurios y mejorar el conocimiento sobre cómo criaban a sus crías. Esta investigación también anima a estudiantes de todo el mundo a interesarse por la paleontología, incluso en lugares sin fósiles de dinosaurios.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al recrear un nido de ovirraptor, los científicos descubrieron que estos dinosaurios usaban tanto su calor corporal como la luz solar para incubar sus huevos, revelando un método único diferente al de las aves modernas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a life-sized, softly textured oviraptor model gently brooding over two concentric rings of smooth, resin-like eggs in a semi-open nest, bathed in gentle sunlight that highlights subtle temperature differences across the eggs, set against a simple natural backdrop with muted earth tones and soft shadows.
